D8 AnthropologyL2 UnderstandReference

Imperial Purple — Roman Sumptuary Law

Emperor Aurelian (270 CE) and later Diocletian restricted Tyrian-purple toga ownership to the emperor; wearing imperial purple without rank carried the death penalty until Justinian (565 CE)

The Roman toga's purple stripe (clavus) signalled rank: thin = equestrian, broad = senator, all-purple = emperor only. Sumptuary codes enforced economic plus social hierarchy. Byzantine emperors continued the practice — wearing imperial purple boots without authorisation cost noses, hands, or lives.

Slide rank to see the legal purple allotment.
